# What is the impact of Small Satellites on Global Connectivity?

#### Write Answer

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/images/satnow-loader.gif)
![](https://cdn.satnow.com/blank-profile-pic.jpg)

Editorial Team - SATNow

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

The progression of satellite technology has been instrumental in shaping global connectivity, fundamentally changing the way people communicate, access information, and interact worldwide. These satellites, positioned approximately 36,000 kilometers above the Earth’s equator, remain fixed relative to the ground, enabling consistent coverage over large areas. These small satellites are transforming the global connectivity landscape, particularly in areas where traditional infrastructure is lacking. This configuration proved highly effective for broadcasting and communication needs, particularly in densely populated and developed regions. Due to their high manufacturing and launch costs, long development timelines, and limited ability to provide low-latency communication, [geostationary satellites](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-is-a-geostationary-orbit) were unable to fully address the connectivity needs of remote, underserved, or economically disadvantaged areas.

The development of small satellites has been catalyzed by advancements in miniaturized electronics, innovative materials, and standardized architectures, such as the CubeSat design, which has significantly reduced costs and simplified the design and launch process. Small satellites offer a multitude of benefits in global connectivity. First, they operate primarily in [Low Earth Orbit (LEO)](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-low-earth-orbits-leo), at altitudes between 300 and 2,000 kilometers, where they can transmit data with lower latency than geostationary satellites. This lower latency is especially critical for applications such as real-time video conferencing, online gaming, and remote sensing, where speed and response time are essential.

The shift toward small satellite deployment is reshaping the connectivity landscape and has implications for various sectors, including education, healthcare, and emergency response. In rural and developing areas, small satellites can deliver essential internet services, closing the digital divide and providing opportunities for economic growth and social inclusion. The financial accessibility of small satellites also democratizes space, allowing smaller countries, startups, and academic institutions to participate in space exploration and technology. Increased traffic in LEO raises concerns about [orbital debris](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-space-debris), which could pose a risk to both operational and future satellites. Additionally, the frequency spectrum required for communication with these satellites is becoming increasingly crowded, necessitating new regulatory approaches to avoid interference and maintain signal integrity. Small satellites are reshaping communication and connectivity, paving the way for a future where internet and mobile access become universally available, creating a more connected and inclusive global community.

**Overview of Small Satellites**

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

Small satellites are a groundbreaking category of satellite technology, have transformed the space industry by providing versatile and cost-efficient solutions for various purposes, including communication, Earth observation, and scientific exploration. Small satellites are categorized primarily by their mass, which defines their overall size, capabilities, and specific use cases.

**Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Its Advantages for Small Satellites**

Most small satellites are deployed in [Low Earth Orbit (LEO)](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-low-earth-orbits-leo), ranging from 300 to 2,000 kilometers above the Earth's surface. LEO is ideal for small satellite missions for several reasons:

**Expanding Use Cases for Small Satellites**

The flexibility, low cost, and scalability of small satellites have broadened their use cases across various sectors:

Small satellites have democratized access to space, enabling more organizations to participate in space-based research and innovation. By lowering costs and simplifying design and deployment, small satellites have opened the doors to a wide range of new applications and have transformed the way we use space technology to connect, observe, and interact with our planet.

**Role of Small Satellites in Global Connectivity**

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

**1. Enabling Broadband Internet Access**

One of the most significant contributions of small satellites to global connectivity is the provision of broadband internet access in areas lacking reliable ground-based infrastructure. Companies like [SpaceX](https://www.satnow.com/companies/1308/spacex) (Starlink), [OneWeb](https://www.satnow.com/companies/1553/oneweb), and Amazon (Project Kuiper) are deploying large-scale constellations of small satellites to create a mesh network in LEO. The ability to provide high-speed internet from space is particularly valuable for rural and developing areas where terrestrial infrastructure is either unavailable or cost-prohibitive. This network provides high-speed, low-latency internet access that rivals traditional broadband but does so from space, bypassing the need for physical infrastructure such as fiber-optic cables, which can be expensive and challenging to deploy, especially in rural or remote locations.

**2. Enhancing Mobile Connectivity**

Small satellites are revolutionizing mobile connectivity by creating new satellite-based cellular networks that can connect directly to standard mobile devices without any need for additional hardware. This capability is particularly transformative for remote and rural regions that traditionally lack cellular coverage, such as isolated islands, mountainous regions, and sparsely populated areas.

**3. Supporting IoT Applications**

The [Internet of Things (IoT)](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-is-satellite-iot) relies on connectivity to transmit data from sensors and devices. For industries such as agriculture, transportation, logistics, and environmental monitoring, small satellites provide a way to maintain IoT connections in remote or mobile settings. Companies like Swarm Technologies and Kepler Communications focus on low-cost, low-data-rate satellite solutions that are ideal for IoT applications.

#### Also Read: [Small Satellites: The Benefits of Downsizing](https://www.satnow.com/community/small-satellites-the-benefits-of-downsizing)

**Technical Advancements in Small Satellite Constellations**

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

Small satellite constellations have benefited from rapid technological advancements that have enhanced their cost-effectiveness, performance, and accessibility. These advancements enable small satellite constellations to deliver reliable, low-latency communication, improved operational efficiency, and enhanced resilience. This section delves into some of the key technical breakthroughs driving the capabilities of small satellite constellations, particularly in the areas of launch accessibility, adaptive architectures, and low-latency communication.

**1. Improved Launch Accessibility**

One of the primary drivers of growth in small satellite constellations is the increased accessibility of launch services. Advancements in satellite miniaturization, CubeSat standardization, and reusable launch vehicles have collectively lowered the cost and increased the frequency of launch opportunities, making space access more feasible for a broader range of organizations.

**2. Adaptive Satellite Constellation Architectures**

Small satellite constellations are designed to function cooperatively, with each satellite playing a role in a larger network. This distributed approach ensures redundancy and resilience, as the failure of a single satellite has a minimal impact on the overall network. Modern satellite constellations leverage advanced inter-satellite links, allowing data to be transmitted between satellites, which improves efficiency and reduces reliance on ground stations.

**3. Low-Latency Communication**

Low latency is a significant advantage of small satellite constellations operating in [LEO](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-low-earth-orbits-leo). By orbiting closer to Earth than traditional geostationary satellites, small satellites can provide faster communication speeds, making them suitable for applications that demand real-time data transmission, such as video conferencing, online gaming, and IoT data analytics.

**Challenges Associated with Small Satellites**

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

While small satellites provide numerous benefits for global connectivity, they also present distinct challenges, particularly as their numbers grow in orbit. Addressing these challenges is essential to ensure that small satellite constellations can operate safely, sustainably, and efficiently. The primary challenges include orbital debris and space traffic management, frequency spectrum and bandwidth limitations, and constraints in power and thermal management.

**1. Orbital Debris and Space Traffic Management**

The rapid growth of small satellite deployments in [Low Earth Orbit (LEO)](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-low-earth-orbits-leo) brings with it the risk of orbital debris and increased congestion in space. As constellations like SpaceX’s Starlink, OneWeb, and others launch thousands of satellites, the potential for collisions and the creation of [space debris](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-are-space-debris) becomes a major issue. Orbital debris poses a serious threat not only to operational satellites but also to the safety and longevity of future missions.

**2. Frequency Spectrum and Bandwidth Limitations**

The increasing number of small satellite constellations presents challenges related to frequency spectrum and bandwidth. Satellites communicate using specific frequencies within the radio spectrum, which is a limited resource. With multiple operators competing for the same spectrum, the risk of interference increases, which can impact the quality and reliability of satellite communication.

**3. Power and Thermal Management**

Small satellites have inherent limitations in power generation, storage, and thermal management due to their compact size. Unlike large satellites, which can house extensive power systems and thermal regulation devices, small satellites must work within size and weight constraints, which affects the types of payloads they can support and the environments they can operate in. Engineers are developing more efficient solar arrays, batteries, and thermal control systems to address these limitations, but these remain a critical area of ongoing research.

Phase-Change Materials (PCMs): PCMs absorb excess heat and release it slowly, helping to maintain a stable temperature within the satellite. When the satellite experiences a temperature spike, the PCM melts, absorbing energy and cooling the interior. During cooler periods, the material re-solidifies, releasing the stored energy.

Thermal Louvers and Radiators: Some small satellites use miniature radiators or thermal louvers that open and close based on temperature conditions. These systems can help radiate excess heat but are limited by the small satellite's surface area.

Advanced Coatings: Thermal coatings can help control heat absorption and radiation, reducing the thermal impact on the satellite. Engineers often apply reflective coatings to certain areas of the satellite to minimize heat absorption from solar radiation, while other surfaces may be designed to dissipate heat efficiently.

Ongoing Research and Development: Power and thermal management continue to be critical areas of research for small satellite engineers. The development of high-efficiency solar cells, lightweight batteries, and innovative thermal control materials is ongoing, with the aim of enhancing the operational capabilities of small satellites. The improvement of these technologies will enable small satellites to support more demanding applications, operate in a wider range of environments, and increase mission longevity.

**Socioeconomic and Environmental Implications**

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

![](https://cdn.satnow.com/website/fixed/gray-svg-image.svg)

Small satellites are transforming global connectivity, making the internet and data access more ubiquitous, especially in underserved regions. This has profound implications for socioeconomic development, environmental monitoring, and privacy and security. As these small satellite networks expand, understanding their broader impacts becomes essential.

**1. Bridging the Digital Divide**

The potential of small satellites to provide global internet access has significant implications for socioeconomic development. By bringing connectivity to rural and isolated areas, small satellites can enable access to education, healthcare, and economic opportunities, thereby helping to reduce the digital divide. Access to the internet can empower individuals in remote regions to access resources, improve literacy, and participate in the global economy.

By providing connectivity in these areas, small satellites help bridge the digital divide, which is a critical step toward more equitable socioeconomic development globally.

**2. Environmental Monitoring and Climate Action**

Small satellites play a crucial role in monitoring environmental changes, such as deforestation, ocean pollution, and climate change. With the data collected from small satellites, scientists can track global environmental trends and provide insights into mitigating climate risks. Enhanced connectivity allows real-time data collection and analysis, aiding in disaster response and resource management.

Through environmental monitoring and resource management, small satellites can help build a more sustainable future. The data they provide is essential for understanding the scope of environmental challenges and designing effective responses to climate change and other ecological threats.

**3. Privacy and Security Concerns**

The widespread deployment of small satellite constellations improves privacy and security concerns. With global, real-time monitoring capabilities, there is an increased risk of data breaches and unauthorized tracking. As more government and private entities deploy small satellites, there is a need for robust cybersecurity measures to protect sensitive information and ensure privacy.

**Future for Small Satellites and Global Connectivity**

With the progression of small satellite technology, the industry is advancing toward even more ambitious goals, such as ultra-low-latency communications, enhanced autonomous capabilities, and integrated hybrid networks that combine space-based connectivity with terrestrial and high-altitude solutions. The development of small satellite technology will likely shape a new era of global connectivity, enabling seamless internet access and data communication to previously unreachable parts of the world. Emerging trends include the development of [Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO)](https://www.satnow.com/community/what-is-very-low-earth-orbit-vleo) satellites, which operate even closer to Earth and provide ultra-low latency communications. Advancements in artificial intelligence and machine learning are enabling satellites to operate more autonomously, improving data processing and decision-making in space. With continued investments and innovations, small satellites are poised to become an integral part of the global communications infrastructure, enabling ubiquitous connectivity and further bridging the gap between connected and unconnected regions. Future developments may also focus on hybrid networks, combining small satellites with terrestrial, high-altitude platform stations (HAPS), and other connectivity solutions to create a seamless and resilient global communication system.

**1. Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) Satellites**

One of the most anticipated advancements in small satellite technology is the development and deployment of Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) satellites, which are positioned even closer to the Earth's surface than traditional Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites, typically at altitudes below 300 kilometers.

**2. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) for Autonomous Satellite Operations**

Artificial intelligence and machine learning are advanced the functionality of small satellites, enabling them to operate autonomously and process data in real time, even from remote orbits.

**3. Integration of Hybrid Networks for Seamless Connectivity**

The future of global connectivity depends on the satellite technology and in the integration of hybrid networks that combine multiple communication platforms, including terrestrial infrastructure, high-altitude platform stations (HAPS), and small satellite constellations.

**4. Continued Investments and Innovations**

The small satellite industry is experiencing unprecedented levels of investment from private companies, governments, and research institutions. This ongoing financial support is driving research and development that will yield the next generation of small satellite technologies.

Small satellites enable global connectivity by providing affordable, scalable, and accessible communication solutions to areas that traditional infrastructure cannot reach. Technical advancements in launch accessibility, adaptive constellation architectures and low-latency communication have made small satellite constellations a viable and valuable resource for global connectivity. Small satellites have introduced new opportunities for global connectivity, but they also bring unique challenges in areas such as orbital debris, frequency spectrum limitations, and power and thermal management. Addressing these challenges requires ongoing innovation, regulatory coordination, and collaborative efforts across the space industry.

The socioeconomic and environmental implications of small satellites are transformative. By bridging the digital divide, they empower remote and underserved communities with access to critical resources, driving educational, healthcare, and economic advancements. The deployment of small satellites is significantly advancing global connectivity by creating new avenues for internet access, mobile connectivity, and IoT applications. By overcoming the limitations of terrestrial infrastructure and addressing the unique challenges of underserved and remote regions, small satellites enable a future where connectivity is more universally accessible.

# Top 10 Benefits of Smaller Satellites in Space

As the space industry continues to grow rapidly into 2024, more and more trends are becoming increasingly apparent as common practice for both public and private groups. One popular trend is the size of the satellites that are orbiting in space. Newer satellites, like phones are becoming smaller and more efficient.

Here are the Top 10 benefits of Smaller satellites, often referred to as smallsats or CubeSats, in space exploration and satellite missions:

### 1. Cost-Effectiveness:

Building and launching smaller satellites is generally more cost-effective compared to larger ones. Smallsats often use standardized components, reducing manufacturing and launch costs.

### **2. Rapid Development and Deployment:**

Smaller satellites can be developed more quickly than larger ones. This allows for faster technology iteration and deployment of new capabilities.

### **3. Agility and Flexibility:**

Smaller satellites can be deployed in constellations, enabling more agile and flexible mission profiles. They can adapt to changing mission requirements and provide more frequent revisits to specific areas on Earth.

### **4. Reduced Launch Complexity:**

Smaller satellites can piggyback on larger satellite launches as secondary payloads, reducing launch complexity and cost. This makes it easier for researchers and smaller organizations to access space.

### **5. Technological Advancements:**

Smaller satellites often leverage advancements in miniaturization and commercial off-the-shelf components. This enables the integration of cutting-edge technologies in a more affordable and compact form.

### **6. Earth Observation and Remote Sensing:**

Small satellites are well-suited for Earth observation missions. Constellations of smallsats can provide high-resolution and frequent imagery for applications such as agriculture, environmental monitoring, and disaster response.

### **7. Scientific Research:**

Smaller satellites are used for scientific research in space, conducting experiments and observations in various fields such as astronomy, atmospheric studies, and space weather monitoring.

### **8. Educational Opportunities:**

CubeSats and other smallsats provide educational institutions with an opportunity to participate in space exploration. Students can gain hands-on experience in designing, building, and operating satellites.

### **9. Risk Mitigation:**

Smaller satellites can be employed for testing new technologies and concepts at a lower risk. If a mission fails, the impact and financial loss are comparatively smaller than with larger, more expensive satellites.

### **10. Global Connectivity:**

Small satellites contribute to the development of global satellite constellations, enabling improved global connectivity for communication purposes, especially in remote or underserved areas.

In summary, the benefits of smaller satellites include cost-effectiveness, rapid development, agility, reduced launch complexity, access to advanced technologies, versatility in applications, and opportunities for education and research.

# Giving small satellites a bigger impact with an advanced wireless chip

by [Institute of Science Tokyo](https://www.isct.ac.jp/en)

This article has been reviewed according to Science X's
[editorial process](https://sciencex.com/help/editorial-process/)

![Transforming small satellites for a bigger impact using an advanced wireless chip](https://scx1.b-cdn.net/csz/news/800a/2025/transforming-small-sat-1.jpg "A CMOS chip with an integrated switch-type quadrature-hybrid and 4-array configuration, mounted on a 64-phase RX array to form a 256-element phased-array receiver (4 array/chip × 64). Credit: Atsushi Shirane/Institute of Science Tokyo, Japan")

The world is steadily moving toward seamless, global connectivity through satellite constellations. Small satellites—weighing up to 10 to 100 kgs—are further enhancing the connectivity with their flexibility and scalability. But the application of small satellites often faces a significant challenge in their ability to accept communication beams.

Satellites communicate using communication beams, which are [electromagnetic waves](https://techxplore.com/tags/electromagnetic+waves/). In some waves, the electric field rotates in a spiral, and these waves are called circularly polarized beams.

Based on the direction of the rotation, the beams can be either in right-hand circular polarization (RHCP) or in left-hand circular polarization (LHCP). Small satellites weighing in the 10s of kgs can only handle single polarization beams, whereas bulkier satellites often require higher power to handle both polarized beams.

Driven by this need, a team led by Associate Professor Atsushi Shirane at Tokyo Institute of Technology, which was integrated into the Institute of Science, in Tokyo, Japan, has successfully developed a novel Ka-band [wireless chip](https://techxplore.com/tags/wireless+chip/) for small satellite communication systems that can independently control the two circularly polarized beams—a property that was unachievable with conventional technologies.

The research was carried out in collaboration with Axelspace, Japan, and the [findings](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10904607/) were presented at the IEEE International Solid-State Circuits Conference ([ISSCC 2025](https://www.isscc.org/)), held from February 16–20, 2025, at the San Francisco Marriott Marquis in California.

"Conventional satellite communication receivers often struggle to handle both RHCP and LHCP beams independently," explains Dr. Shirane. "To overcome this, we designed a switch-type quadrature-hybrid within a wireless chip that can pick up both left-hand and right-hand circularly polarized signals."

![Transforming small satellites for a bigger impact using an advanced wireless chip](https://scx1.b-cdn.net/csz/news/800a/2025/transforming-small-sat.jpg "Traditional small satellites are limited to single-polarization. This new technology allows them to use dual-polarization, effectively doubling their communication capacity and improving performance in remote areas. Credit: Atsushi Shirane/Institute of Science Tokyo, Japan")

A quadrature-hybrid is a special circuit that splits a signal into two parts, with one part delayed slightly to create a 90-degree phase difference. It breaks a circularly polarized signal into two straight signals and allows the chip to compare them. This helps to determine whether the signal was spinning left or right and therefore enables it to recognize both types of polarization used in satellite communication.

The ability to independently steer both types of circularly polarized beams allows for greater communication flexibility, which is a critical requirement for satellite-based networks, especially as demand surges for broadband access in underserved and remote areas. Moreover, this innovation also doubled the number of controllable beams the satellite could handle, significantly improving the system's capacity.

One notable benefit is that the chip has been fabricated using the widely adopted complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS), which is a [low-power](https://techxplore.com/tags/low-power/), fast, and compact technology used to build integrated circuits. This adds to the [cost-effectiveness](https://techxplore.com/tags/cost-effectiveness/) and scalability of the receiver, which is crucial for real-world deployments.

"Our receiver chip works in Ka-band frequency, known for its high-speed data transfer," emphasizes Dr. Shirane. "In fact, it's the very same [frequency band](https://techxplore.com/tags/frequency+band/) harnessed by cutting-edge satellite networks like SpaceX's Starlink."

To verify its performance, the receiver chip was tested within a prototype satellite-mounted communication device and was subjected to over-the-air measurements. This confirmed the chip's performance in handling [circular polarization](https://techxplore.com/tags/circular+polarization/) beams while maintaining the fundamental requirements for satellite communication systems.

The technology is a fundamental leap forward for global connectivity and is expected to have a profound impact on satellite communication infrastructure. Further developments could enable broader high-speed connections, offering coverage across vast geographic areas that were previously unreachable.

In an increasingly connected world, this innovation marks a new chapter for satellite-based communication—one that promises to bridge digital divides and make global communication efficient, affordable, and accessible for all.

# Satellite Contributions to Global Connectivity: Bridging the Digital Divide

![illustration of Skyrora XL](//skyrora.com/wp-content/webp-express/webp-images/uploads/2024/12/RocketsWithDifferentSky_00001-1-1024x576.jpg.webp "Skyrora XL illustration")

In an era where connectivity is as vital as basic utilities, the persistent digital divide remains a significant challenge. Millions of people in remote and underserved regions lack reliable internet access, limiting their opportunities for education, healthcare, and economic growth. Satellite technology has emerged as a transformative solution, capable of bridging this gap by providing robust, wide-reaching connectivity.

### The Role of Satellite Networks in Connectivity

Unlike traditional terrestrial internet infrastructure, which relies on physical cables and networks, satellites can beam internet signals directly to any location on Earth. This capability is especially crucial for remote and rural areas where building physical infrastructure is economically unfeasible or geographically challenging.  
  
Satellite constellations—groups of interconnected satellites in low Earth orbit (LEO), medium Earth orbit (MEO), or geostationary orbit (GEO)—are revolutionizing global connectivity. Companies like SpaceX with Starlink, OneWeb, and Amazon's Project Kuiper are deploying massive satellite networks designed to deliver high-speed internet. These networks can provide coverage in areas that are otherwise "off the grid," including mountain ranges, isolated islands, and disaster zones where terrestrial infrastructure is compromised.

### Bridging the Divide: Socio-Economic Impacts

The implications of satellite-powered connectivity are far-reaching. In education, satellite networks can bring online learning platforms to students in remote locations, enabling them to access the same resources as their urban peers. In healthcare, telemedicine becomes a reality, with rural clinics connecting to specialists in major cities. Agricultural communities can leverage precision farming technologies that rely on real-time data from satellites, optimizing productivity and sustainability.  
  
For businesses, reliable internet connectivity in underserved regions can unlock new markets, create jobs, and drive economic growth. Additionally, governments and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) can use satellite data to monitor natural disasters, support humanitarian aid, and enhance public safety.

### Skyrora’s Vision for a Connected Future

As a forward-thinking player in the space sector, Skyrora is uniquely positioned to contribute to global connectivity solutions. The company specializes in developing sustainable and innovative launch vehicles capable of deploying satellites into orbit from British soil. By supporting the deployment of satellite constellations designed for connectivity, Skyrora plays a pivotal role in addressing the digital divide.  
  
Skyrora's commitment to environmental sustainability further enhances its contribution. The company’s eco-friendly fuels and efficient launch processes align with global efforts to minimize the environmental footprint of space activities. By offering cost-effective, reliable, and sustainable access to orbit, Skyrora empowers satellite providers to extend their networks to the most remote corners of the world.

### Challenges and the Road Ahead

Despite their immense potential, satellite networks face challenges, including high deployment costs, orbital congestion, and latency issues in data transmission. However, advancements in technology, such as inter-satellite laser links and reusable launch systems, are addressing these hurdles. Collaboration among space companies, governments, and international organizations is essential to ensure that satellite connectivity remains accessible and affordable.

## A Transformative Path Forward

Satellite networks are reshaping the global connectivity landscape, breaking barriers and enabling inclusivity in the digital age. By connecting the unconnected, satellites empower communities, improve quality of life, and drive innovation.  
  
Skyrora stands at the forefront of this transformation, providing critical launch services that make these networks possible. As the demand for connectivity grows, Skyrora’s role in delivering satellites to orbit ensures a future where no community is left behind in the digital revolution.  
  
Through continuous innovation and sustainable practices, Skyrora is helping to unlock the full potential of satellite technology—building bridges across the digital divide and inspiring a more connected, equitable world.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Small satellites, typically weighing between 10 to 100 kg, are designed for diverse missions, leveraging advancements in miniaturized technology to increase efficiency and functionality.
Small satellites are more affordable to manufacture and launch due to standardized components, enabling quicker deployment, rapid iteration of technology, and accessible space exploration.

Small satellites enhance global connectivity by providing internet services in remote areas, bridging the digital divide, and supporting critical communication needs where traditional infrastructure is lacking.
Notable missions include SpaceX's Starlink, OneWeb, and Amazon's Project Kuiper, all deploying constellations of small satellites to offer high-speed broadband globally.

enriched_points.point_title: Future trends in small satellite technology
enriched_points.expanded_content: Emerging trends focus on Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) satellites, advanced AI for autonomous operations, and hybrid networks for seamless global connectivity, enhancing communication capabilities.
